is the [distance](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference") around [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Measured around the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), it is 40,075.017 km (24,901.461 mi). Measured passing through the [poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole"), the circumference is 40,007.863 km (24,859.734 mi).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

**Earth's circumference** is the [distance](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference") around [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth"). Measured around the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), it is 40,075.017 km (24,901.461 mi). Measured passing through the [poles](/wiki/Geographical_pole "Geographical pole"), the circumference is 40,007.863 km (24,859.734 mi).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Treating the Earth as a [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere"), its circumference would be its single most important measurement.[[2]](#cite_note-ShekharXiong2007-2) The first known scientific measurement and calculation was done by [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"), by comparing altitudes of the mid-day sun at two places a known north–south distance apart.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He achieved a great degree of precision in his computation.[[4]](#cite_note-russo273277-4) The Earth's shape deviates from spherical by [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening"), but by only about 0.3%.

Measurement of Earth's circumference has been important to [navigation](/wiki/Navigation "Navigation") since ancient times. In modern times, Earth's circumference has been used to define fundamental units of measurement of length: the [nautical mile](/wiki/Nautical_mile "Nautical mile") in the seventeenth century and the [metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre") in the eighteenth. Earth's polar circumference is very near to 21,600 nautical miles because the nautical mile was intended to express one  [minute](/wiki/Minute_and_second_of_arc "Minute and second of arc") of latitude (see [meridian arc](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc")), which is 21,600 partitions of the polar circumference (that is 60 minutes × 360 degrees). The polar circumference is also close to 40,000 kilometres because [the metre was originally defined](/wiki/History_of_the_metre "History of the metre") to be one ten millionth (i.e., a kilometre is one ten thousandth) of the arc from pole to equator ([quarter meridian](/wiki/Quarter_meridian "Quarter meridian")). The accuracy of measuring the circumference has improved since then, but the physical length of each unit of measure had remained close to what it was determined to be at the time, so the Earth's circumference is no longer a [round number](/wiki/Round_number "Round number") in metres or nautical miles.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

See also: [History of geodesy](/wiki/History_of_geodesy "History of geodesy"), [Spherical Earth § History](/wiki/Spherical_Earth#History "Spherical Earth"), [Earth radius § History](/wiki/Earth_radius#History "Earth radius"), and [Meridian arc § History](/wiki/Meridian_arc#History "Meridian arc")

### Eratosthenes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Eratosthenes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Eratosthenes.png/250px-Eratosthenes.png)](/wiki/File:Eratosthenes.png)

Illustration showing a portion of the globe showing a part of the African continent. The sun beams shown as two rays hitting earth at Syene and Alexandria. Angle of sun beam and the gnomons (vertical sticks) is shown at Alexandria which allowed Eratosthenes's estimate of the circumference of Earth.

The measure of Earth's circumference is the most famous among the results obtained by [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) who estimated that the meridian has a length of 252,000 [stadia](/wiki/Stadion_(unit) "Stadion (unit)"), with an error on the real value between −2.4% and +0.8% (assuming a value for the stadion between 155 and 160 metres;[[4]](#cite_note-russo273277-4) the exact value of the stadion remains a subject of debate to this day; see [stadion](/wiki/Stadion_(unit)#Calculations "Stadion (unit)")).

Eratosthenes described his technique in a book entitled *On the measure of the Earth*, which has not been preserved; what has been preserved is the simplified version described by [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes") to popularise the discovery.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Cleomedes invites his reader to consider two Egyptian cities, [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") and Syene (modern [Aswan](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan")):

1. Cleomedes assumes that the distance between Syene and Alexandria was 5,000 stadia (a figure that was checked yearly by professional [bematists](/wiki/Bematist "Bematist"), *mensores regii*).[[7]](#cite_note-7)
2. He assumes the simplified (but inaccurate) hypothesis that Syene was precisely on the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer"), saying that at [local noon](/wiki/Solar_noon "Solar noon") on the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice") the Sun was directly overhead. Syene was actually north of the tropic by something less than a degree.
3. He assumes the simplified (but inaccurate) hypothesis that Syene and Alexandria are on the same meridian. Syene was actually about 3 degrees of longitude east of Alexandria.

According to [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes")'s [*On the Circular Motions of the Celestial Bodies*](/wiki/Cleomedes#On_the_Circular_Motions_of_the_Celestial_Bodies "Cleomedes"), around 240 BC, Eratosthenes calculated the [circumference of the Earth](/wiki/Earth_radius "Earth radius") in [Ptolemaic Egypt](/wiki/Ptolemaic_Kingdom "Ptolemaic Kingdom").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Using a vertical rod known as a [gnomon](/wiki/Gnomon "Gnomon") and under the previous assumptions, he knew that at local noon on the summer solstice in [Syene](/wiki/Syene "Syene") (modern [Aswan](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan"), Egypt), the Sun was directly overhead, as the gnomon cast no shadow. Additionally, the shadow of someone looking down a deep well at that time in Syene blocked the reflection of the Sun on the water. Eratosthenes then measured the Sun's angle of elevation at noon in Alexandria by measuring the length of another gnomon's shadow on the ground.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Using the length of the rod and the length of the shadow as the legs of a triangle, he calculated the angle of the sun's rays.[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10) This angle was about 7°, or 1/50th the circumference of a [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle"); assuming the Earth to be perfectly spherical, he concluded that its circumference was 50 times the known distance from Alexandria to Syene (5,000 stadia, a figure that was checked yearly), i.e. 250,000 stadia.[[11]](#cite_note-stade-11) Depending on whether he used the "Olympic stade" (176.4 m) or the Italian stade (184.8 m), this would imply a circumference of 44,100 km (an error of 10%) or 46,100 km, an error of 15%.[[11]](#cite_note-stade-11) A value for the stadion of 157.7 metres has even been posited by L.V. Firsov, which would give an even better precision, but is plagued by calculation errors and false assumptions.[[12]](#cite_note-engels-12) In 2012, Anthony Abreu Mora repeated Eratosthenes's calculation with more accurate data; the result was 40,074 km, which is 66 km different (0.16%) from the currently accepted polar circumference.[[10]](#cite_note-:6-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg/500px-Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg)

Measure of Earth's circumference according to Cleomedes's simplified version, based on the approximation that [Syene](/wiki/Aswan "Aswan") is on the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") and on the same meridian as [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria").

Eratosthenes's method was actually more complicated, as stated by the same Cleomedes, whose purpose was to present a simplified version of the one described in Eratosthenes's book. Pliny, for example, has quoted a value of 252,000 stadia.[[13]](#cite_note-Pliny-13)

The method was based on several [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") trips conducted by professional [bematists](/wiki/Bematist "Bematist"), whose job was to precisely measure the extent of the territory of Egypt for agricultural and taxation-related purposes.[[4]](#cite_note-russo273277-4) Furthermore, the fact that Eratosthenes's measure corresponds precisely to 252,000 stadia (according to Pliny) might be intentional, since it is a number that can be divided by all natural numbers from 1 to 10: some historians believe that Eratosthenes changed from the 250,000 value written by Cleomedes to this new value to simplify calculations;[[14]](#cite_note-14) other historians of science, on the other side, believe that Eratosthenes introduced a new length unit based on the length of the meridian, as stated by Pliny, who writes about the stadion "according to Eratosthenes' ratio".[[4]](#cite_note-russo273277-4)[[13]](#cite_note-Pliny-13)

### Posidonius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Posidonius")]

Main article: [Posidonius § Earth's circumference](/wiki/Posidonius#Earth's_circumference "Posidonius")

[Posidonius](/wiki/Posidonius "Posidonius") calculated the Earth's circumference by reference to the position of the star [Canopus](/wiki/Canopus_(star) "Canopus (star)"). As explained by [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes"), Posidonius observed Canopus on but never above the horizon at [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"), while at [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") he saw it ascend as far as 7+1⁄2 degrees above the horizon (the [meridian arc](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") between the latitude of the two locales is actually 5 degrees 14 minutes). Since he thought Rhodes was 5,000 [stadia](/wiki/Stadion_(unit_of_length) "Stadion (unit of length)") due north of Alexandria, and the difference in the star's elevation indicated the distance between the two locales was 1/48 of the circle, he multiplied 5,000 by 48 to arrive at a figure of 240,000 stadia for the circumference of the earth.[[15]](#cite_note-fragment_202-15) It is generally thought[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] that the stadion used by Posidonius was almost 1/10 of a modern statute mile.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Thus Posidonius's measure of 240,000 stadia translates to 24,000 mi (39,000 km), not much short of the actual circumference of 24,901 mi (40,074 km).[[15]](#cite_note-fragment_202-15) [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") noted that the distance between Rhodes and Alexandria is 3,750 stadia, and reported Posidonius's estimate of the Earth's circumference to be 180,000 stadia or 18,000 mi (29,000 km).[[16]](#cite_note-16) [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") mentions Posidonius among his sources and—without naming him—reported his method for estimating the Earth's circumference. He noted, however, that [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus") had added some 26,000 stadia to Eratosthenes's estimate. The smaller value offered by Strabo and the different lengths of Greek and Roman stadia have created a persistent confusion around Posidonius's result. [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") used Posidonius's lower value of 180,000 stades (about 33% too low) for the earth's circumference in his [*Geography*](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)"). This was the number used by [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") in order to underestimate the distance to India as 70,000 stades.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Aryabhata

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aryabhata")]

Around AD 525, the Indian mathematician and astronomer wrote *[Aryabhatiya](/wiki/Aryabhatiya "Aryabhatiya")*, in which he calculated the diameter of earth to be of 1,050 *[yojanas](/wiki/Yojanas "Yojanas")*. The length of the *yojana* intended by Aryabhata is in dispute. One careful reading gives an equivalent of 14,200 kilometres (8,800 mi), too large by 11%.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Another gives 15,360 km (9,540 mi), too large by 20%.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Yet another gives 13,440 km (8,350 mi), too large by 5%.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Islamic Golden Age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Islamic Golden Age")]

Around AD 830, [Caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") [Al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun") commissioned a group of [Muslim astronomers](/wiki/Muslim_astronomers "Muslim astronomers") led by [Al-Khwarizmi](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_Musa_al-Khwarizmi "Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi") to measure the distance from Tadmur ([Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra "Palmyra")) to [Raqqa](/wiki/Raqqa "Raqqa"), in modern [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"). They calculated the Earth's circumference to be within 15% of the modern value, and possibly much closer. How accurate it actually was is not known because of uncertainty in the conversion between the medieval Arabic units and modern units, but in any case, technical limitations of the methods and tools would not permit an accuracy better than about 5%.[[21]](#cite_note-HOC2_1-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg/250px-Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg)

Diagram showing how al-Biruni was able to calculate the Earth's circumference by measuring the dip of the horizon from a point at a known height.

A more convenient way to estimate was provided in [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni")'s *Codex Masudicus* (1037). In contrast to his predecessors, who measured the Earth's circumference by sighting the Sun simultaneously from two locations, [al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") developed a new method of using [trigonometric](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") calculations, based on the angle between a [plain](/wiki/Plain "Plain") and [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") top, which made it possible for it to be measured by a single person from a single location.[[21]](#cite_note-HOC2_1-21) From the top of the mountain, he sighted the [dip angle](/wiki/Horizon#Zenith_angle "Horizon") which, along with the mountain's height (which he determined beforehand), he applied to the [law of sines](/wiki/Law_of_sines "Law of sines") formula. This was the earliest known use of dip angle and the earliest practical use of the law of sines.[[22]](#cite_note-Savizi-22) However, the method could not provide more accurate results than previous methods, due to technical limitations, and so al-Biruni accepted the value calculated the previous century by the [al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun") expedition.[[21]](#cite_note-HOC2_1-21)

### Columbus's error

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Columbus's error")]

Main article: [Christopher Columbus § Quest for Asia](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus#Quest_for_Asia "Christopher Columbus")

1,700 years after Eratosthenes's death, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") studied what Eratosthenes had written about the size of the Earth. Nevertheless, based on a map by [Toscanelli](/wiki/Toscanelli "Toscanelli"), he chose to believe that the Earth's circumference was 25% smaller. If, instead, Columbus had accepted Eratosthenes's larger value, he would have known that the place where he made landfall was not [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), but rather a [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World").[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Historical use in the definition of units of measurement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Earth%27s_circumference&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical use in the definition of units of measurement")]

Main article: [History of the metre § Meridional definition](/wiki/History_of_the_metre#Meridional_definition "History of the metre")

Further information: [Meridian arc § History of measurement](/wiki/Meridian_arc#History_of_measurement "Meridian arc"), [Earth radius § History](/wiki/Earth_radius#History "Earth radius"), and [Arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain](/wiki/Arc_measurement_of_Delambre_and_M%C3%A9chain "Arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain")

In 1617 the Dutch scientist [Willebrord Snellius](/wiki/Willebrord_Snellius "Willebrord Snellius") assessed the circumference of the Earth at 24,630 Roman miles (24,024 statute miles). Around that time British mathematician [Edmund Gunter](/wiki/Edmund_Gunter "Edmund Gunter") improved navigational tools including a new [quadrant](/wiki/Quadrant_(instrument) "Quadrant (instrument)") to determine latitude at sea. He reasoned that the lines of latitude could be used as the basis for a unit of measurement for [distance](/wiki/Distance "Distance") and proposed the nautical mile as one minute or one-sixtieth (⁠1/60⁠) of one [degree](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") of latitude. As one degree is ⁠1/360⁠ of a circle, one minute of arc is ⁠1/21600⁠ of a circle – such that the polar circumference of the Earth would be exactly 21,600 miles. Gunter used Snellius's circumference to define a nautical mile as 6,080 feet, the length of one minute of arc at 48 degrees latitude.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 1793, France defined the metre so as to make the polar circumference of the Earth 40,000 kilometres. To measure this distance accurately, the [French Academy of Sciences](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences") commissioned [Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Joseph_Delambre "Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre") and [Pierre Méchain](/wiki/Pierre_M%C3%A9chain "Pierre Méchain") to lead [an expedition](/wiki/Arc_measurement_of_Delambre_and_M%C3%A9chain "Arc measurement of Delambre and Méchain") to attempt to accurately measure the distance between a belfry in [Dunkerque](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk") and [Montjuïc castle](/wiki/Montju%C3%AFc_Castle_(Barcelona) "Montjuïc Castle (Barcelona)") in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") to estimate the length of the [meridian arc](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") through Dunkerque. The length of the first [prototype metre bar](/wiki/Metre#International_prototype_metre_bar "Metre") was based on these measurements, but it was later determined that its length was short by about 0.2 millimetres because of the miscalculation of the [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of the Earth, making the prototype about 0.02% shorter than the original proposed definition of the metre. Regardless, this length became the French standard and was progressively adopted by other countries in Europe.[[25]](#cite_note-Alder2003-25) This is why the polar circumference of the Earth is actually 40,008 kilometres, instead of 40,000.
